summary: Gruber explores the line between inspiration and rip-off in the context of the Wordle phenomenon and its imitators. He establishes practical criteria for what constitutes a rip-off: stealing the name, attempting to confuse consumers, or bringing nothing new. He examines the claim that Wordle ripped off the TV game show Lingo, finding the comparison weak, and discovers that the official Lingo app is a dystopian free-to-play nightmare. He contrasts Wordle's elegant simplicity with Lingo's aggressive monetization, then surveys several Wordle-inspired games, distinguishing between legitimate derivative works and actual rip-offs.
key_points: Three practical tests for rip-offs: stealing the name, trying to pass as the original, or bringing nothing new to the table

The official Lingo app undermines its own rip-off claims against Wordle by being an aggressively monetized, poorly designed game with unskippable ads, dual currencies, and mandatory bingo between levels
Wordle's genius is in its restraint — free, simple, one puzzle per day — while Lingo represents everything wrong with free-to-play mobile gaming
Derivative works exist on a spectrum: Word Game Hero and Saltong show how to draw inspiration gracefully with proper attribution, while Zach Shakked's clone was shameless
Physical media and video rental stores offered better access to obscure films than today's streaming landscape
money_quotes: Some good rules of thumb, if you're weighing whether a derivative new work crosses the threshold into ripping off the original: If the derivative steals the original's title or name, that's a rip-off.
If the derivative is designed to confuse people into thinking it is the original — as Shakked's Wordle clone clearly was — that's a rip-off.
Lingo might not be the worst game on the App Store, but it's the worst and most oppressively dystopic game I've ever played.
Josh Wardle's Wordle is a wonderfully simple, totally free game designed only to bring people a bit of serene enjoyment for a few minutes per day.

The official Lingo app is an ugly cacophonous confusing jumble of concepts intended to hook players on in-app purchases, and whose only saving grace is that it's no fun at all to actually play and thus, although not for lack of trying, not the least bit addictive in practice.
There's a reason you probably never heard of the official Lingo app.
It strikes me as wrong that any game with in-app purchases should be rated for young children. Real-world casinos don't have slot machines for children; the App Store shouldn't either.
It's bananas that it was easier to find 'old' movies on physical media at my beloved local video store 20 years ago than it is today on streaming platforms and online stores.
key_insight: The difference between inspiration and rip-off comes down to intent and craft: derivative works that bring something new, credit their sources, and don't try to deceive are legitimate, while those that steal names and confuse consumers are not.
